critique.summary: The article presents the source code and discussion for a Drugwars game adapted for TI-82/83/83+ calculators, originally based on an IBM BASIC game by John E. Dell. It includes user comments about compatibility, bug fixes, and enhancements for TI calculators, as well as insights into the game's mechanics and programming challenges.
mainPoints: The provided code is a TI-BASIC program simulating the Drugwars game for TI-82/83/83+ calculators.
The game is based on an original IBM BASIC game by John E. Dell, with gameplay involving buying and selling drugs to pay off debt and make money.
Users have discussed compatibility issues and bug fixes for TI-84+ and TI-84 Plus CE calculators, including modifications to random encounters and game mechanics.
The code reuses variables in ways that can be exploited for gameplay advantages, such as unlimited bank/loan shark access and free heroine.
The source code and game logic have been reverse-engineered and cleaned up from older versions, with community contributions for improvements and ports to other platforms.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are accurate and verifiable. The Drugwars game is a known classic text-based game originally written in BASIC, and the TI-BASIC code shown corresponds to a legitimate port for TI calculators. User comments and developer notes align with known facts about TI calculator programming and the game's history. The discussion about variable reuse and exploits is consistent with typical programming quirks in TI-BASIC. The article does not make unsupported or sensational claims and provides multiple user perspectives and technical details.
considerations: The article does not mention the ethical implications of simulating illegal drug trade in a game, which may be a concern for some audiences.
There is no official endorsement or licensing information about the original game or this port, which may affect legal considerations.
The technical complexity of entering or transferring the code to calculators may be a barrier for casual users.
The article does not provide detailed instructions for users unfamiliar with TI-BASIC programming or calculator file transfers.
Potential compatibility issues with newer calculator models or operating systems are noted but not comprehensively addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the nostalgic value, programming experiences, and technical aspects of the Drugwars game on TI calculators. Participants share personal stories, technical modifications, and related projects, highlighting its impact on their learning and creativity.

comments.author: TimTheTinker
comments.text:
TI-83 Basic was the first programming language I really felt like I had mastered. For a while in my first CS college class I was writing code in TI basic and translating it to C++. Drugwars and Bowling were the two really impressive games written in TI-Basic.

But discovering z80 assembly was like magic. It was incredibly exciting to go to my dad's office at the university where he worked (where computers had 2 T1 internet lines) to download and try assembly games when they first burst on the scene (I was in 8th grade). Bill Nagel blew my mind with Turbo Breakout and Snake, and later AShell, Penguins, and grayscale Mario... but the best executed and most replayable games I think were Sqrxz and ZTetris on the TI-86 by Jimmy Mardell. Honorable mention to Galaxian and Falldown. I once downloaded the z80 assembly source for a game, printed it to about an inch of paper, and carried it around for weeks trying to understand it...

It was also really cool for some reason (and would often brick the calculator until you took the batteries out) to type random hex pairs into a program and execute it as assembly. "C063" run as assembly - syntax was the random looking Send(9PrgmA where PrgmA is where you typed the hex code - on a TI-83 would scroll tons of random text in an infinite loop.

Does anyone remember the TI website wars? TI Files (later TI Philes) was "so much more awesome" than "the lowly weak ticalc.org"... but look which one is still around :-)
comments.analysis: Provides a detailed personal history emphasizing the educational value of TI-Basic and early programming experiences, adding depth to the discussion.

comments.author: jckahn
comments.text:
This game is a really big deal for me! I was addicted to it in high school and it left a lasting impression. Drugwars directly inspired my passion project, Farmhand: https://www.farmhand.life/

I'm so happy to see this pop up here! :)
comments.analysis: Highlights the game's influence on personal development and creativity, illustrating its cultural significance.

comments.author: qaid
comments.text:
My "fun fact" that I always tell is that I got my start by reading the manual of my TI-83+

I spent most of my 9th grade making a stick figure clone of Street Fighter, using TI-BASIC and graphing functions.

Eventually I switched to coding with pencil and paper because the calculator screen can only show you 8 lines at a time. No idea how I made something that could support 2 players playing on the same calculator, all with GOTOs and LABELs.

My favorite optimization of all time was turning their heads into hexagons instead of circles since drawing 6 lines was so much faster.
comments.analysis: Shows how early curiosity and experimentation with TI calculators fostered programming skills, enriching the discussion.

comments.author: montroser
comments.text:
I had a friend in ninth grade in the late 1900s who was a talented artist. He used his skills to make beautifully expressive pixel-art hardcore pornography on the TI-82.

He crafted a few different scenes, where for each one, he set it to loop back and forth between two frames -- and the implied motion was fantastically realistic for the resolution and fps he was working with...
comments.analysis: Provides an unusual but authentic anecdote about the creative and rebellious uses of calculator programming.

comments.author: no-name-here
comments.text: For those unfamiliar, the game started in 1984 on DOS: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Drug_Wars_(video_game)
comments.analysis: Offers historical context, grounding the discussion in the game's origins and evolution.

comments.author: voxadam
comments.text:
It's also available on the far superior HP 48 series (presumably the 49/50 and Prime as well).

https://www.hpcalc.org/details/911

All hail RPN!
comments.analysis: Mentions a different platform without adding substantive insight into the TI calculator context.

comments.author: mrhyyyyde
comments.text: First experiences around programming were on an 83, I'll never forget those choose your own adventure games I let friends play in class.
comments.analysis: Expresses nostalgia and personal experience, contributing to the collective memory of TI programming.

comments.author: aklein
comments.text: I wrote a clone of this game for the HP-48 as a teen in the 90s. you can still find it if you google hard enough. good times.
comments.analysis: Adds to the narrative of the game's influence and the community's ongoing projects.

comments.author: makeramen
comments.text: Oh man, I ported this to the TI-89 back in 7th grade and made it slightly more school appropriate calling it “pop wars”, trading soda from different machines at different schools instead of drugs.
comments.analysis: Demonstrates creative adaptation and humor, enriching the discussion of the game's cultural impact.

comments.author: radicaldreamer
comments.text:
Still a classic set of z80 apps including a symbolic equations solver for the TI-83. I played and used the hell out of these in high school.

MirageOS was the iPhone Home Screen of that time.

https://detachedsolutions.com/main/
comments.analysis: Highlights the technical versatility of TI calculators, broadening the scope of the discussion.
